eli3: Word2Vec (Mikolov et al. 2013) learns dense vector representations (embeddings) for words such that semantically similar words have similar vectors: vector('king') − vector('man') + vector('woman') ≈ vector('queen'). The famous analogy test. The key insight: words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings — learn representations by predicting context from word (Skip-gram) or word from context (CBOW).

There are two architectures: Skip-gram predicts surrounding context words given the center word; CBOW predicts the center word from surrounding context words. Both are trained with negative sampling: instead of computing a softmax over all V words, sample k negative (non-context) words and train a binary classifier. This reduces training cost from O(V) to O(k) per word — critical for vocabularies of 100K+.

Word2Vec produces static embeddings: 'bank' has the same embedding regardless of context (river bank vs bank account). This was a major limitation, addressed by contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT). However, Word2Vec embeddings remain useful for: fast inference, low-resource scenarios, and as initialization for neural models. Pre-trained embeddings (GoogleNews 300d, CommonCrawl) are available.

whenWhy.title: Semantic similarity and word clustering
whenWhy.body: Word2Vec embeddings capture semantic and syntactic regularities: king−man+woman≈queen (semantic analogy), fast−faster≈slow−slower (comparative pattern), Paris−France+Germany≈Berlin (country-capital). Cosine similarity between vectors measures semantic relatedness: cosine(cat, dog) > cosine(cat, table). Used for: synonym detection, word sense disambiguation (using nearest neighbors), semantic search (query expansion with similar words).

whenWhy.title: Initialization for downstream NLP models
whenWhy.body: Word2Vec embeddings serve as pre-trained initialization for neural NLP models, replacing random initialization. Pre-training on large corpora (100B words) captures general semantic knowledge — fine-tuning on task-specific data then adapts these representations. This was the standard approach before BERT: embed tokens with Word2Vec/GloVe, encode with LSTM, classify. Transfer learning from pre-trained word embeddings provides significant gains on small datasets.

whenWhy.title: Fast inference and low-resource NLP
whenWhy.body: Word2Vec produces 100–300 dimensional static embeddings that are extremely fast at inference (just a lookup table). For production systems with strict latency budgets or IoT/edge devices, Word2Vec + simple classifiers outperform transformers. For low-resource languages, Word2Vec can be trained on small corpora (10M words) and still provides useful representations.

deepDiveIntro: Skip-gram objective: maximize Σ_{(w,c) ∈ D} log P(c|w) where P(c|w) = exp(vw·uc) / Σv exp(vw·uv). Negative sampling approximation: log σ(vw·uc) + Σk~Pn log σ(−vw·uk), where Pn(w) = f(w)^{3/4}/Z (unigram distribution raised to 3/4 power — samples frequent words as negatives more often but with dampening). Each word has two vectors: center word vector v, context word vector u.

CBOW: predict center word w from context words {c₁,…,c_{2k}}: maximize P(w|c₁,…,c_{2k}) ≈ P(w|mean(uc_i)). Faster than Skip-gram (fewer operations per word). Skip-gram: for each center word, predict each context word separately — more updates per word, better for rare words. In practice: Skip-gram with negative sampling (SGNS) gives better embeddings than CBOW and is the standard.

deepDive.heading: Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS)
deepDive.body: For each (center word w, context word c) pair within window size k: minimize binary cross-entropy: −log σ(vw·uc) − Σᵢ₌₁ᴷ log σ(−vw·unᵢ). Negative samples nᵢ drawn from Pn(w) ∝ f(w)^{3/4}. k = 5–20 negatives typically. Hyperparameters: vector dimension d = 100–300, window size = 5–10, Pn exponent = 0.75, subsampling frequent words (P(discard) = 1 − √(t/f(w))). Training: SGD or Adam. O(k·d) per word pair. [1]
deepDive.formula: L_SGNS = log σ(vw · uc) + Σₖ log σ(−vw · unₖ)

deepDive.heading: Linear Structure and Analogical Reasoning
deepDive.body: The geometric meaning of word vectors: offsets between vectors encode semantic relations. king − man ≈ queen − woman (gender relation), Paris − France ≈ Berlin − Germany (capital-country). This linear structure arises because PMI (Pointwise Mutual Information) matrix factorization and SGNS are equivalent (Levy & Goldberg 2014): SGNS implicitly factorizes the shifted PMI matrix PMI(w,c) − log k. The analogy offset is a linear encoding of the relational semantic difference. [1]

deepDive.heading: Subsampling and Rare Word Handling
deepDive.body: Frequent words ('the', 'a', 'is') appear too often — subsampling: discard word w with probability P(discard) = 1 − √(t/f(w)), threshold t = 10⁻⁵. This reduces training data while keeping semantic content. Rare words: min_count = 5 (default) — words appearing < 5 times are discarded. OOV words get no embedding — a limitation vs subword models. For better rare word coverage: FastText (character n-grams within words) extends Word2Vec. [1]

deepDive.heading: Static vs Contextual Embeddings
deepDive.body: Word2Vec assigns one vector per word form — identical for all usages. 'bank' has the same vector regardless of 'river bank' vs 'bank account'. This was fixed by contextual embeddings: ELMo (Peters et al. 2018) uses BiLSTM representations that change with context; BERT uses self-attention. For tasks sensitive to word sense disambiguation (WSD), coreference resolution, and complex semantic reasoning, contextual embeddings are necessary. Word2Vec remains useful as a fast, lightweight alternative when compute is constrained. [2]

takeaways: Skip-gram: predict context from center word; CBOW: predict center from context. SGNS is the standard. [1]
Negative sampling: train binary classifier vs k random negatives — reduces cost from O(V) to O(k). [1]
SGNS ≡ implicit factorization of shifted PMI matrix — explains linear analogy structure. [1]
Limitation: static embeddings — 'bank' has one vector regardless of context (river vs financial). [2]
FastText extends Word2Vec with character n-grams, handling OOV words by composing subword vectors. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does Word2Vec use two separate embedding matrices (center vs context)?
edgeCases.a: Each word w has a center vector vw (used when w is the center word) and a context vector uw (used when w is a context word). These capture different roles. After training, the center vectors are typically used as word representations; context vectors are discarded. Using a single shared matrix would enforce vw = uw, which is mathematically possible but empirically gives worse results — the asymmetry between center and context roles helps the optimization landscape. Some implementations add vw + uw as the final representation. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you evaluate word embeddings beyond analogy tasks?
edgeCases.a: Analogy evaluation (Google analogies, MSR analogies) has been criticized for being too sensitive to the specific arithmetic used. Alternative evaluations: (1) Word similarity benchmarks (SimLex-999, WordSim-353) — cosine similarity should correlate with human judgments; (2) Downstream task performance (NER, sentiment, parsing accuracy when embeddings are used as features); (3) Intrinsic linguistic probing (syntactic probes, morphological probes). The field has moved toward evaluating embeddings via downstream task performance rather than intrinsic similarity benchmarks. [1]

sources.label: Mikolov et al. (2013) — Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality, NeurIPS 2013
Peters et al. (2018) — Deep Contextualized Word Representations (ELMo), NAACL 2018
